dialogue.utterance: 9-1-1, this is dispatch. Send unit to possible stroke at [COMPANY_NAME] in [CITY].
Hey, I'm Jason, an EMT with [COMPANY_NAME] EMS. What brings you to call 911?
Can you tell me what's going on with you today?
I've been having weakness on my right side for a few days. I think I had a stroke.
Can you tell me your name?
I don't recall, but I think it starts with H.
Your eyes are open and alert, but you seem a little confused.
Let me clarify that. Your eyes are open, and you've responded to voice but appear disoriented.
What's the current date and year?
I don't remember. Maybe around 2020?
I'm holding up my hand. Can you match me? You're right arm is drifting.
When you say you fell, how did that happen?
I fell out of bed. I'm not sure how long ago.
Do you have any medical history we should know about?
I've had strokes in the past.
Are you taking any medications?
No, I'm not taking anything currently.
Any known allergies?
None.
What's the last thing you ate or drank?
I haven't eaten or had anything to drink in the last few days.
Are you experiencing any pain right now?
No, I don't feel any pain at the moment.
Head and neck examination shows no deformities or swelling.
No jugular venous distension or tracheal deviation present.
Eyes are open spontaneously; pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light (PERRL).
No drainage or bleeding from the ears, nose, or mouth observed.
Speech is clear and without distortion despite your concern about it being slurred earlier.
No signs of lymphadenopathy or nuchal rigidity found during head and neck scan.
Pulse is 84, with normal rhythm.
Respiratory rate is 18, with normal depth and rate consistent with baseline breathing.
Blood pressure is 161 over 81, with no signs of acute hypertension.
Initial oxygen saturation displayed 0%, so we're rechecking with the pulse oximeter quickly.
Oxygen saturation is now showing 100%, confirming normal oxygen levels after recheck.
A finger stick shows a glucose reading of 104 mg/dL, within normal range for blood sugar levels.
We're placing you on continuous stroke monitoring per protocol with a 4-lead EKG setup now.
The initial EKG shows a normal sinus rhythm with no ST changes or arrhythmias detected at this time.
An 18-gauge IV has been successfully started in your left arm for potential fluids or medications if needed during transport.
Right arm drift was observed, along with decreased grip strength and reported numbness on the right side of the body.
Skin is warm, pink, and dry with no presence of rashes, bites, or other skin irregularities noted.
Chest movement is equal on both sides with no signs of respiratory distress or labored breathing observed at this time.
Lungs are clear bilaterally. No wheezes, crackles, or pleural rubs detectable with auscultation at this point in time.
Let me recheck your vitals now. Blood pressure is 164 over 76 now, indicating no dramatic changes.
Pulse rate is currently 83, still regular without tachycardia or bradycardia observed so far.
Your respiratory rate has risen to 22 with same normal depth; shall keep this on observation as per protocol.
Let's confirm the oxygen saturation reading again before proceeding. Reading is solidly 100% on 2nd test per device.
Blood pressure is now recording 168 over 92, showing some elevation in diastolic and systolic pressures combined.
Final review prior to transport shows a heart rate of 86 beats per minute, still within an acceptable range for protocol tracking purposes.
Your Glasgow Coma Scale score is 14, breaking down with Eyes 4, Verbal 4, and Motor 6 indicating no significant neurological degeneration per field assessment reviews today .
A 12-lead EKG was recently completed in the field showing a sinus rhythm deemed normal for a male older than 40 persons by standard interpretive reviews today .
We followed Protocol 3-5 for medical stroke/TIA in the field to ensure correct baseline responses before transport decisions were made among units active onsite then .
We will now initiate emergent transport to [COMPANY_NAME] for further neurological assessment and potential stroke imaging workflow reviews tomorrow .
You have been safely placed onto the stretcher using a 5-point harness for transport secureness and care practices enforced that day .
ED team, we're transferring a 72-year-old male with reported right-side weakness consistent with possible stroke symptoms is concerning transport group .
Last recorded SpO2 at 98% before transport initiation shows normal oxygenation levels assessed for clearance upon arrival at facility networks .
